Results for VDBA Scaling Analysis

O.W.

2025-11-10

Methods

  • 37 species datasets of raw tri-axial accelerometer data.
  • Formatted to standard data structure
  • Smoothed outliers with a butterworth filter (cutoff_freq, 0.5 Hz, to remove only sudden spikes)
  • Generated dynamic VDBA by removing static acceleration (either as a rolling average of 10 seconds of VDBA for continuous data or as the average of the burst)
  • Separated instances into active and inactive categories such that all instances with a rolling standard deviation (measure of flatness in the rolling window) in the botton 25% were classed as inactive.
  • Summarised max and mean VDBA per individual.

Individual Analysis

Where the masses of the exact individual are known. Per species plot and then all known individuals on the same graph.

## Linear mixed model fit by REML. t-tests use Satterthwaite's method [
## lmerModLmerTest]
## Formula: log10(meanVDBA) ~ LogMass * threshold + (1 | Dataset)
##    Data: all_data
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: -89.3
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -5.5891 -0.4290 -0.0447  0.5352  4.1605 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups   Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  Dataset  (Intercept) 0.29455  0.5427  
##  Residual             0.03402  0.1844  
## Number of obs: 258, groups:  Dataset, 6
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                            Estimate Std. Error        df t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)                -0.20698    0.43219  41.73102  -0.479  0.63450    
## LogMass                    -0.04977    0.09044 202.61349  -0.550  0.58275    
## thresholdall               -0.08476    0.17999 247.17973  -0.471  0.63810    
## thresholdinactive          -1.97168    0.17999 247.17973 -10.954  < 2e-16 ***

## LogMass:thresholdinactive   0.23255    0.04410 247.17973   5.274 2.92e-07 ***
## ---

## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##                (Intr) LogMss thrshldl thrshldn LgMss:thrshldl

## thresholdll    -0.208  0.241                                 
## thrshldnctv    -0.208  0.241  0.500                          
## LgMss:thrshldl  0.206 -0.244 -0.988   -0.494                 
## LgMss:thrshldn  0.206 -0.244 -0.494   -0.988    0.500

Single system analysis

Where exact masses are not included (species averages) but all data is collected using the same accelerometer sampling protocol.Repeated for all Clemente datasets and for all Buchmann datasets. # V1: Axivity (Clemente)

## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

All species, with approximate/average masses.

And then only the files where the system didn’t max out.

## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Curvi-linear trend

Chris says: “I wonder if you picked only the highest VDBA for each body mass, and used a GAMS model to predict the pattern, would it be hump shaped? with a peak around 50kg? A curvilinear trend might also explain why sometimes we see a positive slope (e.g. with quolls, small size) and a negative slope with kangaroos (large body size)”
